screens.prompt: A signal repeats exactly every $T_0$ seconds — genuinely periodic, not just "roughly similar" like your burst's statistics will turn out to be. Can any such periodic signal always be built by adding up complex exponentials spinning at $0, \pm f_0, \pm 2f_0, \pm 3f_0, \ldots$ (integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/T_0$), for suitable amplitudes?
choices: No — only signals that already look like sine waves can be built this way
Yes — this is the Fourier series: essentially any periodic signal (under mild technical conditions) decomposes exactly into harmonics of its fundamental frequency
screens.reveal: Yes — this is one of the most consequential facts in all of signal processing. Any period-$T_0$ signal (again, under mild conditions almost every real signal satisfies) can be written as $x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} c_k\, e^{j2\pi k f_0 t}$, a sum of complex exponentials at every integer multiple of the fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/T_0$. The $c_k$ are the **Fourier series coefficients** — a complete, exact re-encoding of the periodic signal as "how much of each harmonic frequency is present."

screens.prompt: A periodic signal repeats every $T_0 = 0.02$ s. What is its fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/T_0$, in Hz?
screens.successFeedback: Right — 1 / 0.02 = 50 Hz. Every harmonic in this signal's Fourier series sits at an integer multiple of 50 Hz: 0, ±50, ±100, ±150 Hz, and so on.
hints: Fundamental frequency is the reciprocal of the period: f₀ = 1/T₀.

screens.prompt: In $x(t) = \sum_k c_k\, e^{j2\pi k f_0 t}$, what does the coefficient $c_k$ represent?

choices.text: How much of the k-th harmonic (frequency k·f₀) is present in the signal, as a complex amplitude carrying both size and phase
The signal's value at time t = k
choices.feedback: c_k doesn't depend on t at all — it's a fixed number (for a fixed signal) describing the whole harmonic's contribution, not a value at any particular instant.

choices.text: The total energy of the signal at all frequencies combined
choices.feedback: Each individual c_k describes just one harmonic's contribution; the total signal's energy comes from combining information across every c_k, not from any single one.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — each c_k is a complex number: its magnitude is that harmonic's amplitude, and its angle is that harmonic's phase offset.

screens.body: How do you actually extract a single $c_k$ out of the infinite sum? The trick is **orthogonality**: for integers $m \neq k$, $\int_0^{T_0} e^{j2\pi m f_0 t}\,e^{-j2\pi k f_0 t}\,dt = 0$, while for $m = k$ the same integral equals $T_0$ exactly (the integrand becomes 1 everywhere). Multiply both sides of the Fourier series by $e^{-j2\pi k f_0 t}$ and integrate over one period: every term in the sum except $k$ itself integrates to zero, leaving $\int_0^{T_0} x(t)\,e^{-j2\pi k f_0 t}\,dt = c_k T_0$. Rearranging gives the coefficient formula: $c_k = \dfrac{1}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0} x(t)\,e^{-j2\pi k f_0 t}\,dt$ — multiply the signal by the harmonic you're looking for, and average.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why does multiplying x(t) by e^{-j2πkf₀t} and integrating over one period isolate exactly the c_k term, killing off every other term in the sum?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that after multiplying, each term in the sum becomes c_m·e^{j2π(m-k)f₀t}. Integrating any such term over one full period gives zero whenever m ≠ k (the orthogonality property — a complex exponential at any nonzero integer multiple of the fundamental frequency averages to zero over a full period), and gives exactly c_k · T₀ when m = k (since the exponential becomes 1 everywhere). So every term except the one you're targeting vanishes, leaving only c_k behind.

screens.worked: For $x(t) = 3 + 2\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$, rewrite the cosine as phasors: $x(t) = 3 + e^{j2\pi f_0 t} + e^{-j2\pi f_0 t}$ (using $2\cos(\theta) = e^{j\theta}+e^{-j\theta}$). Matching against $\sum_k c_k e^{j2\pi kf_0t}$ term by term: $c_0 = 3$ (the constant/DC term), $c_1 = 1$, $c_{-1} = 1$, and every other $c_k = 0$.
screens.prompt: For $x(t) = 5 + 4\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$, what is $c_1$ (the coefficient of the $e^{j2\pi f_0 t}$ term)?
screens.successFeedback: Right — 4cos(θ) = 2e^{jθ} + 2e^{-jθ}, so c₁ = 2 (and c₋₁ = 2, c₀ = 5, every other c_k = 0). The pattern from the worked example generalizes directly: an A·cos term always splits into two phasors each of amplitude A/2.
hints: Match the coefficient of e^{j2πf₀t} directly against c₁.
